courses.title: Introduction to Computational Thinking for Arts and Social Science Students
courses.description: An introduction to computational thinking and its applications to the arts and social sciences. Students will gain computational thinking skills by exploring data representation, basic programming concepts, a selection of algorithms, and advanced usage of software packages for the arts and social sciences.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science I
courses.description: Introduction to computer science and programming. Topics include: algorithm design; control structures; variables and types; linear collections; functions; debugging and testing. Special attention is given to procedural programming in a modern language, computational thinking skills, and problem decomposition.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science II
courses.description: A second course in programming emphasizing problem solving and computational thinking in an object-oriented language. Topics include abstraction, mutable data structures, methods, inheritance, polymorphism, recursion, program efficiency, testing and debugging.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405.

courses.title: Math for Game Programmers
courses.description: Math for building 3D games. Points, vectors, normals. Dot and cross products. Transformations and inverses in left- and right-handed systems. Uses for controlling objects, cameras, and texture manipulation. Bounding boxes, planes, frustums for collision detection and visibility, fast billboarding techniques, point and sphere sweeping. Quaternions.
courses.prereqRaw: one Grade 12 university preparation mathematics course.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science I
courses.description: Introduction to computer science and programming, for computer science students. Topics include: algorithm design; control structures; variables and types; linear collections; functions; debugging and testing. Special attention is given to procedural programming in a modern language, computational thinking skills, and problem decomposition.
courses.prereqRaw: restricted to students registered in the B.C.S. program, B.Cyber. program, B.D.S. program, combined Honours in Computer Science and Mathematics, Honours Computer Mathematics, and Honours Computer Statistics.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science II
courses.description: A second course in programming for BCS students, emphasizing problem solving and computational thinking in an object-oriented language. Topics include abstraction, mutable data structures, methods, inheritance, polymorphism, recursion, program efficiency, testing and debugging.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405. Restricted to students registered in the B.C.S. program, B.Cyber. program, B.D.S. program, combined Honours in Computer Science and Mathematics, Honours Computer Mathematics, and Honours Computer Statistics.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Game Design
courses.description: Introduction to game design and prototyping. Topics include: formal theories of fun; the mechanics-dynamics-aesthetics framework; game economies; game balance; statistical tools for analyzing game mechanics; game settings; and storytelling. Special attention is given to the attributes of games and what makes a game fun.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405.

courses.title: Introduction to Mobile Application Development
courses.description: Introduction to developing mobile applications using the Mac OS X platform. Topics include: the Objective-C programming language; development tools; framework API's; and the Quartz graphic system. Extensive practical experience with development for Apple mobile devices such as the iPhone.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405.

courses.title: Discrete Structures I
courses.description: Introduction to discrete mathematics and discrete structures. Topics include: propositional logic, predicate calculus, set theory, complexity of algorithms, mathematical reasoning and proof techniques, recurrences, induction, finite automata and graph theory. Material is illustrated through examples from computing.
courses.prereqRaw: one Grade 12 university preparation mathematics course.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. This course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: Permission of the School and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: User Interaction Design and Prototyping
courses.description: Introduction to the principles of interaction design, including the human-centred design process, creative ideation, requirements gathering, prototyping, ethical considerations in design, rapid usability evaluation, and iterative design in a variety of user interaction paradigms.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Applied Cryptography and Authentication
courses.description: Practical aspects of cryptography. Topics include: stream and block ciphers; modes of operation; hash functions; message and user authentication; authenticated key establishment protocols; random number generation; entropy; proof of knowledge; secret sharing; key distribution; pitfalls deploying public-key encryption and digital signatures.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-, and COMP 2804.

courses.title: Introduction to Security and Privacy
courses.description: A tour of Internet security and privacy. Societal impacts and case studies. Topics from: protection goals of stakeholders; history of public key cryptography; programming languages and security; security engineering and testing; cybercrime and malware; Internet privacy and anonymity; government surveillance; regulation; ethics; blockchain applications.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-, and COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Introduction to Systems Programming
courses.description: Introduction to system-level programming with fundamental OS concepts, procedures, primitive data types, user-defined types. Topics may include process management, memory management, process coordination and synchronization, inter-process communication, file systems, networking, pointers, heap and stack memory management, and system/library calls.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Abstract Data Types and Algorithms
courses.description: Introduction to the design and implementation of abstract data types and to complexity analysis of data structures. Topics include: stacks, queues, lists, trees and graphs. Special attention is given to abstraction, interface specification and hierarchical design using an object-oriented programming language.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Introduction to Software Engineering
courses.description: Introduction to object-oriented software development, with emphasis on the design and implementation of maintainable, reusable software. Topics include abstraction, modularity, encapsulation, and an introduction to design patterns.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Web Applications
courses.description: Introduction to Internet application development; emphasis on computer science fundamentals of technologies underlying web applications. Topics include: scripting and functional languages, language-based virtual machines, database query languages, remote procedure calls over the Internet, and performance and security concerns in modern distributed applications.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Computer Game Design and Development
courses.description: Introduction to the practical development of computer games and engine architecture. Topics include: vector and matrix operations; coordinate systems and transformations; physical simulation; collision detection; AI; path planning; hardware-accelerated real-time rendering. Special attention is given to implementation of real-time rendering in a low-level language.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Mobile Applications
courses.description: Development of applications for mobile environments taking advantage of gesture-based input and using location and presence services. Topics include introduction to low-level network services and mobile platforms, description of architectural patterns, principles of mobile development and interaction styles for network service usage.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1601.

courses.title: Introduction to Robotics
courses.description: A course on programming simulated mobile robots with various sensors such as wheel encoders, distance sensors, cameras, compasses, accelerometers, and laser range finders. Topics include: programming robot behaviour; performing position estimation; implementing algorithms related to navigation, mapping, path planning, area coverage, and localization.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Discrete Structures II
courses.description: A second course in discrete mathematics and discrete structures. Topics include: counting, sequences and sums, discrete probability, basic statistics, recurrence relations, randomized algorithms. Material is illustrated through examples from computing.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, or permission of the School of Computer Science for those in Combined Honours in Computer Science and Mathematics.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1911 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Operating Systems
courses.description: Operating system implementation course stressing fundamental issues in design and how they relate to modern computer architectures. Assignments involve the modification and extension of a multitasking operating system.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C- and COMP 2402.

courses.title: Compiler Construction
courses.description: The structure, organization and design of the phases of a compiler are considered: lexical translators, syntactical translators, scope handlers, type checkers, code generators and optimizers. Components of a compiler will be implemented.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402.

courses.title: Object-Oriented Software Engineering
courses.description: Development of object-oriented software systems: theory and practice. Topics include: Computer ethics, software development processes, requirement specification, class and scenario modeling, state modeling, UML, design patterns, traceability. Students are to complete a team project.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) with a minimum grade of C-, and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Database Management Systems
courses.description: Introduction to the concepts of database management systems and database design. Topics may include: entity-relationship modelling, normalization, reduction to relational schemas, SQL, file and data organization, indexing, hash tables, join algorithms, query processing, query optimization, and concurrency control.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, and either COMP 2402 or (SYSC 2004 and SYSC 2100).

courses.title: Programming Paradigms
courses.description: An introduction to alternative programming paradigms such as functional, constraint-based, concurrent, and logic programming.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2402, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Software Structures for User Interfaces
courses.description: Concepts and principles related to building user interfaces, and applications in implementing interfaces in “front-end” programming contexts. Topics may include: reactive programming, input and output factors, application interfaces and infrastructure, typical patterns used to implement them, and organization and management of these aspects within software.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
courses.description: An introduction to methods for automated learning of relationships on the basis of empirical data. Includes topics in supervised and unsupervised machine learning and deeper knowledge of specific algorithms and their applications. Evaluation and quantification of performance of ML systems. Discussion of data ethics.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 2804 and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
courses.description: Principles and tools used in Artificial Intelligence. Fundamentals of Knowledge Representation and Reinforcement Learning and Nature-Based computing. Methods for non-adversarial problem solving including non-exhaustive and heuristic-based strategies for searching the state space. Methods for adversarial problem solving, modeled as two-person and multi-person games.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 2804.

courses.title: Principles of Computer Networks
courses.description: This is an introductory course to the field of Network Computing. Topics include: Protocol Architectures and Internetworking, Types of Networks, Communication Protocols, End-System and Network Traffic Management, Structure of Routing and Congestion Control.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, and COMP 2402.

courses.title: Technical Writing for Computer Science
courses.description: Technical communication for computer science majors, concentrating on writing scientific papers and technical reports. Principles of clarity and precision in writing and communication. Practical exercises and readings from recent technical publications will be used.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110).

courses.title: Computational Logic and Automated Reasoning
courses.description: Applications of formal logic in computer science. Symbolic logics such as classical predicate calculus are used to represent domain knowledge, to model computational problems and to solve them by means of automated reasoners. Applications include artificial intelligence, software engineering, data management and hardware verification.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804.

courses.title: Foundations of Game Programming and Computer Graphics
courses.description: The theory and practice of 3D graphics for computer games. Topics include: vectors and quaternions; hierarchical transformations; camera and perspective; hardware-accelerated real-time rendering; texture and texture mapping; illumination; and particle systems. Additional topics may include rigid-body motion, character animation, shadows, and screen-space special effects.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110).

courses.title: Introduction to Theory of Computation
courses.description: Theoretical aspects of computer science. Topics include: formal languages and automata theory, computability theory.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804.

courses.title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms I
courses.description: An introduction to the design and analysis of algorithms. Topics include: divide-and-conquer, dynamic programming, linear programming, greedy algorithms, graph algorithms, NP-completeness.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and one of (COMP 2804 or COMP 3805 or MATH 3825 or MATH 3855).

courses.title: Discrete Structures and Applications (Honours)
courses.description: Enumeration: inclusion and exclusion, recurrence relations, generating functions and applications. Graph theory: connectivity, planarity, Hamilton paths and Euler trails. Error-correcting codes. Designs and finite geometries. Symmetry and counting.
courses.prereqRaw: MATH 2100 or a grade of B or higher in MATH 2108 or MATH 3101.

courses.title: Mathematical Software
courses.description: Implementation of numerical methods using numerical software packages. Development of scientific and/or operations research applications using application programming interfaces of numerical or optimization libraries. Functional programming for data analysis and machine learning. Experience working with Python, C++, or Java is essential.
courses.prereqRaw: A grade of C- or higher in MATH 3806.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2911 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Distributed Operating Systems
courses.description: An advanced course on the software infrastructure supporting large-scale cloud computing applications. Topics may include: distributed file systems, distributed databases, overlay networks, container orchestration, coordination services, security and privacy services, and large-scale AI pipelines.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001) and (COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602).

courses.title: Distributed Computing
courses.description: Overview of distributed computing. Topics include: computational models, communication complexity, design and analysis of distributed algorithms and protocols, fault-tolerant protocols, synchronous computations. Applications may include: communication in data networks, control in distributed system (e.g., election, distributed mutual exclusion), manipulation of distributed data (e.g., ranking).
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Real-Time 3D Game Engines
courses.description: The design and implementation of game engines for real-time 3D games including topics such as camera control, environmental effects, articulated models, terrain, vegetation, collision detection, particles, emitters, triggers, portals, waypoints, mirrors, and shadows.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110.

courses.title: Transaction Processing Systems
courses.description: Concepts and architectures of transaction processing systems and on-line transaction processing, with emphasis on data integration systems. Transaction properties and models, embedded-SQL, active rules, consistency maintenance, serializability, concurrency control, recovery, data integration systems and federated databases, introduction to transactions in web services and workflow systems.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 3005.

courses.title: Software Quality Assurance
courses.description: Introduction to the theory and practice of Software Quality Assurance. Topics include: equivalence partitioning, test-driven testing, unit testing patterns, refactoring, software metrics, requirements engineering, scenario modeling and acceptance testing, model-based testing, state machine testing, software testing theory and tools.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3004.

courses.title: Evaluation and Research Methods for Human-Computer Interaction
courses.description: Fundamental Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) research and evaluation methods. Topics may include: HCI research methodologies, research ethics, expert evaluation, user studies, qualitative and quantitative data collection, statistical data analysis, information visualization, and specifying practical implications of findings.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2008, COMP 3008, and STAT 2509 with a minimum grade of C- in each.

courses.title: Programming for Clusters and Multi-Core Processors
courses.description: Introduction to parallel architectures, programming languages and algorithms for processor clusters and multi-core processors. Distributed memory architectures, cluster computing, message passing parallel programming, multi-core processors, shared memory parallel programming, use of thread libraries, parallel performance analysis.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 2804.

courses.title: Introduction to Reinforcement Learning
courses.description: Learn about designing and programming reinforcement learning agents to perform complex tasks in interactive environments. Topics include Markov decision processes, dynamic programming methods, Monte Carlo methods, temporal difference learning, prediction/control with function approximation, policy gradient, and deep reinforcement learning algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110), (MATH 1004 or MATH 1007), (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107), and (DATA 1517 or STAT 2507).

courses.title: Computer Vision
courses.description: The basic ideas and techniques of computer vision. The central theme is reconstructing 3D models from 2D images. Topics include: image formation, image feature extraction, camera models, camera calibration, structure from motion, stereo, recognition, augmented reality, image searching.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Neural Networks
courses.description: An introduction to neural networks and deep learning. Theory and application of Neural Networks to problems in machine learning. Various network architectures will be discussed. Methods for improving optimization and generalization of neural networks. Neural networks for unsupervised learning.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3105 or SYSC 4415) and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Computer Systems Security
courses.description: Information security in computer and communications systems. Topics include: design principles; operating system security and access control; web and software security; malicious software, security infrastructure; secure email; network authentication; firewalls; intrusion detection; IP security; network attacks; wireless security.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2108 and (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001).

courses.title: Data Management for Business Intelligence
courses.description: Application of computational techniques to support business activities, such as decision making, business understanding, data analysis, business process automation, learning from data, producing and using data-centric business models, ontology-based data access and integration, data quality assessment and cleaning and use of contextual data.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3005.

courses.title: Quantum Computing and Information
courses.description: Introduction to the ideas and principles of quantum computing and information. Review of mathematical foundations. Discussion of quantum theory, architecture, and quantum gates. Basic algorithms in quantum computing. Theoretical computer science and computation. Applications of quantum computing to cryptography. Quantum information and error correction.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804.

courses.title: Introduction to Natural Language Processing
courses.description: Introduction to the fundamental techniques and models of modern natural language processing. Topics include: word embedding, language models, machine translation, self-attention and transformer, question answering, and pre-trained models.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3105 or SYSC 4415) and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Multiagent Systems
courses.description: Multiagent systems is a branch of artificial intelligence that explores the interactions between multiple rational entities, where each may have access to different information and possibly conflicting priorities. This course takes an approach founded on economic game theory.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3106.

courses.title: Computational Aspects of Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Through recent advances in navigation systems, mobile devices, and new software such as Mapquest and Google Earth, GIS is becoming increasingly important and exciting from a CS perspective. This course lays the algorithmic foundations to understand, use and further this technology.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3804 or MATH 3804.

courses.title: Wireless Networks and Security
courses.description: An introduction to wireless networks covering both networking issues and security aspects of modern wireless environments. Fundamentals of mobile LANs, ad hoc, sensor networks, secure routing, searching, clustering, multicasting, localization, mobile IP/TCP, confidentiality, key establishment, authentication, broadcasting, RFIDs, and rogue attacks.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602.

courses.title: Evolving Information Networks
courses.description: Convergence of social and technological networks. Interplay between information content, entities creating it and technologies supporting it. Structure and analysis of such networks, models abstracting their properties, techniques link analysis, search, mechanism design, power laws, cascading, clustering and connections with work in social sciences.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805, (COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Advanced Facilities for Real-Time Games
courses.description: A practical course on the design and implementation of modern game engines and advanced facilities provided by these engines. Such facilities include systems for rendering 3D scenes; simulating physics; playing animations; game AI; and enabling multi-player games. Students will undertake a significant game development project.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3501.

courses.title: Computer Graphics
courses.description: An overview of computer graphics, with an emphasis on rendering. Topics include the following: visibility; ray tracing; graphics hardware; object and scene modeling; lighting, shading, and texture; animation.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2402, (MATH 1004 or MATH 1007), and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Intelligent Web-based Information Systems
courses.description: Introduction to the principles and practice of creation, delivery and analysis of multimedia content in web-based systems. Topics include analysis of webs of documents, social network analysis, recommender systems and problems of trust, reputation and influence in e-commerce systems.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or COMP 2601 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Social Networking
courses.description: Introduction to virtual communities, overlay networks and social networking. Topics include architectural principles for heterogeneous social networking platforms, trust and reputation as social concepts, agent-based computing, and extraction of trends and patterns from information exchanged between community members.
courses.prereqRaw: ((COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504)) or COMP 2601.

courses.title: Computing, Society, and Ethics
courses.description: This course examines ethical questions raised by computing technologies - both motivated by recent developments and through the lens of fiction. Students will identify possible ethical issues in future technologies and use formal ethics frameworks to evaluate the merits and pitfalls of different solutions.
courses.prereqRaw: Any two of COMP 2108, COMP 3004, COMP 3005, COMP 3008, (COMP 3105 or SYSC 4415), COMP 3106, COMP 3804.

courses.title: Algorithms for Data Mining, Web, and Social Networks
courses.description: Algorithm design techniques for modern data sets arising in, for example, data mining, web analytics, search engines and social networks. Topics may include locality-sensitive hashing, data streaming, clustering, recommendation systems, link analysis, dimensionality reduction, online algorithms, social network analysis, graph partitioning, and randomized algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804 with a minimum grade of B+.

courses.title: Computable Functions
courses.description: Recursive functions and computability, algorithms, Church's thesis, Turing machines, computational logic, NP-completeness.
courses.prereqRaw: MATH 2100 or COMP 3805 or permission of the School.

courses.title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms II
courses.description: A second course on the design and analysis of algorithms. Topics include: advanced recurrence relations, algebraic complexity, advanced graph algorithms, amortized analysis, algorithms for NP-complete problems, randomized algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3804 or MATH 3804.

courses.title: Theory of Automata
courses.description: Finite automata and regular expressions, properties of regular sets, context-free grammars, pushdown automata, deterministic context-free languages. Turing machines, the Chomsky hierarchy. Undecidability, intractable problems.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3805 or MATH 3106 or MATH 3158 (or MATH 3100) or permission of the School.

courses.title: Scientific Computing II (Honours)
courses.description: Computational methods in numerical linear algebra including solvers for large linear systems, numerical optimization and eigenvalue problems. Application to topics such as scientific machine learning or parallel computing. Focus on implementation in an efficient compiled language and standard numerical libraries.
courses.prereqRaw: i) MATH 3806; and ii) MATH 2152 or MATH 2107 with a grade of C- or higher, or permission of the school.

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science
courses.description: Advanced topics in Computer Science offered by members of the School of Computer Science.
courses.prereqRaw: permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Directed Studies
courses.description: Independent study under the supervision of a member of the School of Computer Science. Students are required to obtain their supervisor's written approval prior to registration and are limited to two such courses in their program.
courses.prereqRaw: permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Honours Project
courses.description: Under the supervision of a faculty member, Honours students complete a major Computer Science project in fourth year. Permission to register is granted once an approved project proposal is submitted to the Department. See deadlines and details on the School website.
courses.prereqRaw: fourth-year standing in an Honours program and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Honours Thesis
courses.description: Independent research under the direct supervision of a faculty advisor. Permission to register is granted once an approved thesis proposal is submitted to the School of Computer Science. Evaluation is based on a written thesis and a poster presentation.
courses.prereqRaw: fourth-year standing in an Honours program with a minimum CGPA of 9.0 in the major and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3911 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 4910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Cryptographic Algorithms and Protocols
courses.description: Block ciphers and modes of operation; public-key encryption; cryptographic hash functions; digital signatures; password-based cryptography; randomness and guesswork; authentication and authenticated key establishment protocols; challenge-response protocols; elliptic curve cryptography; post-quantum algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-, (COMP 2804 or STAT 2507), and MATH 1104.

courses.title: Systems Security
courses.description: Securing networked computer systems. Threat modelling. Operating system security and design principles. Access control. Software-based exploits, memory safety, non-functional testing in software development. Social engineering. Browser-server and transport-layer security. Middleperson attacks, end-to-end security. Public-key certificates. Case study: Bluetooth or Wi-Fi security.
courses.prereqRaw: CSEC 2108, (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001), and (COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602).

courses.title: Operating Systems Security
courses.description: The course examines past, present, and emerging approaches for securing operating systems. The focus is to provide a foundation for understanding requirements to secure hosts at the operating system level and survey the landscape of available tools and techniques for implementing operating system security controls.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001) and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Human Factors in Security
courses.description: Designing security mechanisms with human factors in mind. Evaluating software-based systems with focus on how interaction design affects security/privacy. Current approaches to usable security; user studies; methodologies for empirical analysis; design principles for usable security/privacy; case studies including authentication, anonymity systems.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3008 and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Network Security
courses.description: Security throughout network stack layers. Internet core security. VPNs and tunnelling protocols. Firewalls and Intrusion Detection Systems. Internet measurements. IoT security. Botnets. Securing network protocols, including email and web. Network monitoring. Traffic sniffers and vulnerability scanners.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001), (COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602), and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Software Security
courses.description: Resilience of everyday software to vulnerabilities. Security engineering and the security development lifecycle. Static analysis and vulnerability analysis. Model checkers. Security testing, non-functional testing, fuzz-testing. Programming languages and security. Cryptographic APIs and use of security toolkits.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001), COMP 3004 and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Selected Topics in Security I
courses.description: An in-depth study of selected topics, with an emphasis on areas of strong current interest in research or practice.
courses.prereqRaw: CSEC 3108, and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Selected Topics in Security II
courses.description: An in-depth study of selected topics, with an emphasis on areas of strong current interest in research or practice.
courses.prereqRaw: CSEC 3108, and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Introduction to Computational Thinking for Arts and Social Science Students
courses.description: An introduction to computational thinking and its applications to the arts and social sciences. Students will gain computational thinking skills by exploring data representation, basic programming concepts, a selection of algorithms, and advanced usage of software packages for the arts and social sciences.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science I
courses.description: Introduction to computer science and programming. Topics include: algorithm design; control structures; variables and types; linear collections; functions; debugging and testing. Special attention is given to procedural programming in a modern language, computational thinking skills, and problem decomposition.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science II
courses.description: A second course in programming emphasizing problem solving and computational thinking in an object-oriented language. Topics include abstraction, mutable data structures, methods, inheritance, polymorphism, recursion, program efficiency, testing and debugging.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405.

courses.title: Math for Game Programmers
courses.description: Math for building 3D games. Points, vectors, normals. Dot and cross products. Transformations and inverses in left- and right-handed systems. Uses for controlling objects, cameras, and texture manipulation. Bounding boxes, planes, frustums for collision detection and visibility, fast billboarding techniques, point and sphere sweeping. Quaternions.
courses.prereqRaw: one Grade 12 university preparation mathematics course.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science I
courses.description: Introduction to computer science and programming, for computer science students. Topics include: algorithm design; control structures; variables and types; linear collections; functions; debugging and testing. Special attention is given to procedural programming in a modern language, computational thinking skills, and problem decomposition.
courses.prereqRaw: restricted to students registered in the B.C.S. program, B.Cyber. program, B.D.S. program, combined Honours in Computer Science and Mathematics, Honours Computer Mathematics, and Honours Computer Statistics.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Science II
courses.description: A second course in programming for BCS students, emphasizing problem solving and computational thinking in an object-oriented language. Topics include abstraction, mutable data structures, methods, inheritance, polymorphism, recursion, program efficiency, testing and debugging.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405. Restricted to students registered in the B.C.S. program, B.Cyber. program, B.D.S. program, combined Honours in Computer Science and Mathematics, Honours Computer Mathematics, and Honours Computer Statistics.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Game Design
courses.description: Introduction to game design and prototyping. Topics include: formal theories of fun; the mechanics-dynamics-aesthetics framework; game economies; game balance; statistical tools for analyzing game mechanics; game settings; and storytelling. Special attention is given to the attributes of games and what makes a game fun.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405.

courses.title: Introduction to Mobile Application Development
courses.description: Introduction to developing mobile applications using the Mac OS X platform. Topics include: the Objective-C programming language; development tools; framework API's; and the Quartz graphic system. Extensive practical experience with development for Apple mobile devices such as the iPhone.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1005 or COMP 1405.

courses.title: Discrete Structures I
courses.description: Introduction to discrete mathematics and discrete structures. Topics include: propositional logic, predicate calculus, set theory, complexity of algorithms, mathematical reasoning and proof techniques, recurrences, induction, finite automata and graph theory. Material is illustrated through examples from computing.
courses.prereqRaw: one Grade 12 university preparation mathematics course.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. This course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: Permission of the School and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: User Interaction Design and Prototyping
courses.description: Introduction to the principles of interaction design, including the human-centred design process, creative ideation, requirements gathering, prototyping, ethical considerations in design, rapid usability evaluation, and iterative design in a variety of user interaction paradigms.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Applied Cryptography and Authentication
courses.description: Practical aspects of cryptography. Topics include: stream and block ciphers; modes of operation; hash functions; message and user authentication; authenticated key establishment protocols; random number generation; entropy; proof of knowledge; secret sharing; key distribution; pitfalls deploying public-key encryption and digital signatures.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-, and COMP 2804.

courses.title: Introduction to Security and Privacy
courses.description: A tour of Internet security and privacy. Societal impacts and case studies. Topics from: protection goals of stakeholders; history of public key cryptography; programming languages and security; security engineering and testing; cybercrime and malware; Internet privacy and anonymity; government surveillance; regulation; ethics; blockchain applications.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-, and COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Introduction to Systems Programming
courses.description: Introduction to system-level programming with fundamental OS concepts, procedures, primitive data types, user-defined types. Topics may include process management, memory management, process coordination and synchronization, inter-process communication, file systems, networking, pointers, heap and stack memory management, and system/library calls.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Abstract Data Types and Algorithms
courses.description: Introduction to the design and implementation of abstract data types and to complexity analysis of data structures. Topics include: stacks, queues, lists, trees and graphs. Special attention is given to abstraction, interface specification and hierarchical design using an object-oriented programming language.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Introduction to Software Engineering
courses.description: Introduction to object-oriented software development, with emphasis on the design and implementation of maintainable, reusable software. Topics include abstraction, modularity, encapsulation, and an introduction to design patterns.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Web Applications
courses.description: Introduction to Internet application development; emphasis on computer science fundamentals of technologies underlying web applications. Topics include: scripting and functional languages, language-based virtual machines, database query languages, remote procedure calls over the Internet, and performance and security concerns in modern distributed applications.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Computer Game Design and Development
courses.description: Introduction to the practical development of computer games and engine architecture. Topics include: vector and matrix operations; coordinate systems and transformations; physical simulation; collision detection; AI; path planning; hardware-accelerated real-time rendering. Special attention is given to implementation of real-time rendering in a low-level language.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Mobile Applications
courses.description: Development of applications for mobile environments taking advantage of gesture-based input and using location and presence services. Topics include introduction to low-level network services and mobile platforms, description of architectural patterns, principles of mobile development and interaction styles for network service usage.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1601.

courses.title: Introduction to Robotics
courses.description: A course on programming simulated mobile robots with various sensors such as wheel encoders, distance sensors, cameras, compasses, accelerometers, and laser range finders. Topics include: programming robot behaviour; performing position estimation; implementing algorithms related to navigation, mapping, path planning, area coverage, and localization.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.title: Discrete Structures II
courses.description: A second course in discrete mathematics and discrete structures. Topics include: counting, sequences and sums, discrete probability, basic statistics, recurrence relations, randomized algorithms. Material is illustrated through examples from computing.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, or permission of the School of Computer Science for those in Combined Honours in Computer Science and Mathematics.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1911 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Operating Systems
courses.description: Operating system implementation course stressing fundamental issues in design and how they relate to modern computer architectures. Assignments involve the modification and extension of a multitasking operating system.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C- and COMP 2402.

courses.title: Compiler Construction
courses.description: The structure, organization and design of the phases of a compiler are considered: lexical translators, syntactical translators, scope handlers, type checkers, code generators and optimizers. Components of a compiler will be implemented.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402.

courses.title: Object-Oriented Software Engineering
courses.description: Development of object-oriented software systems: theory and practice. Topics include: Computer ethics, software development processes, requirement specification, class and scenario modeling, state modeling, UML, design patterns, traceability. Students are to complete a team project.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) with a minimum grade of C-, and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Database Management Systems
courses.description: Introduction to the concepts of database management systems and database design. Topics may include: entity-relationship modelling, normalization, reduction to relational schemas, SQL, file and data organization, indexing, hash tables, join algorithms, query processing, query optimization, and concurrency control.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, and either COMP 2402 or (SYSC 2004 and SYSC 2100).

courses.title: Programming Paradigms
courses.description: An introduction to alternative programming paradigms such as functional, constraint-based, concurrent, and logic programming.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2402, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Software Structures for User Interfaces
courses.description: Concepts and principles related to building user interfaces, and applications in implementing interfaces in “front-end” programming contexts. Topics may include: reactive programming, input and output factors, application interfaces and infrastructure, typical patterns used to implement them, and organization and management of these aspects within software.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
courses.description: An introduction to methods for automated learning of relationships on the basis of empirical data. Includes topics in supervised and unsupervised machine learning and deeper knowledge of specific algorithms and their applications. Evaluation and quantification of performance of ML systems. Discussion of data ethics.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 2804 and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
courses.description: Principles and tools used in Artificial Intelligence. Fundamentals of Knowledge Representation and Reinforcement Learning and Nature-Based computing. Methods for non-adversarial problem solving including non-exhaustive and heuristic-based strategies for searching the state space. Methods for adversarial problem solving, modeled as two-person and multi-person games.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 2804.

courses.title: Principles of Computer Networks
courses.description: This is an introductory course to the field of Network Computing. Topics include: Protocol Architectures and Internetworking, Types of Networks, Communication Protocols, End-System and Network Traffic Management, Structure of Routing and Congestion Control.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, and COMP 2402.

courses.title: Technical Writing for Computer Science
courses.description: Technical communication for computer science majors, concentrating on writing scientific papers and technical reports. Principles of clarity and precision in writing and communication. Practical exercises and readings from recent technical publications will be used.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110).

courses.title: Computational Logic and Automated Reasoning
courses.description: Applications of formal logic in computer science. Symbolic logics such as classical predicate calculus are used to represent domain knowledge, to model computational problems and to solve them by means of automated reasoners. Applications include artificial intelligence, software engineering, data management and hardware verification.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804.

courses.title: Foundations of Game Programming and Computer Graphics
courses.description: The theory and practice of 3D graphics for computer games. Topics include: vectors and quaternions; hierarchical transformations; camera and perspective; hardware-accelerated real-time rendering; texture and texture mapping; illumination; and particle systems. Additional topics may include rigid-body motion, character animation, shadows, and screen-space special effects.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110).

courses.title: Introduction to Theory of Computation
courses.description: Theoretical aspects of computer science. Topics include: formal languages and automata theory, computability theory.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804.

courses.title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms I
courses.description: An introduction to the design and analysis of algorithms. Topics include: divide-and-conquer, dynamic programming, linear programming, greedy algorithms, graph algorithms, NP-completeness.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and one of (COMP 2804 or COMP 3805 or MATH 3825 or MATH 3855).

courses.title: Discrete Structures and Applications (Honours)
courses.description: Enumeration: inclusion and exclusion, recurrence relations, generating functions and applications. Graph theory: connectivity, planarity, Hamilton paths and Euler trails. Error-correcting codes. Designs and finite geometries. Symmetry and counting.
courses.prereqRaw: MATH 2100 or a grade of B or higher in MATH 2108 or MATH 3101.

courses.title: Mathematical Software
courses.description: Implementation of numerical methods using numerical software packages. Development of scientific and/or operations research applications using application programming interfaces of numerical or optimization libraries. Functional programming for data analysis and machine learning. Experience working with Python, C++, or Java is essential.
courses.prereqRaw: A grade of C- or higher in MATH 3806.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2911 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Distributed Operating Systems
courses.description: An advanced course on the software infrastructure supporting large-scale cloud computing applications. Topics may include: distributed file systems, distributed databases, overlay networks, container orchestration, coordination services, security and privacy services, and large-scale AI pipelines.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001) and (COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602).

courses.title: Distributed Computing
courses.description: Overview of distributed computing. Topics include: computational models, communication complexity, design and analysis of distributed algorithms and protocols, fault-tolerant protocols, synchronous computations. Applications may include: communication in data networks, control in distributed system (e.g., election, distributed mutual exclusion), manipulation of distributed data (e.g., ranking).
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Real-Time 3D Game Engines
courses.description: The design and implementation of game engines for real-time 3D games including topics such as camera control, environmental effects, articulated models, terrain, vegetation, collision detection, particles, emitters, triggers, portals, waypoints, mirrors, and shadows.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110.

courses.title: Transaction Processing Systems
courses.description: Concepts and architectures of transaction processing systems and on-line transaction processing, with emphasis on data integration systems. Transaction properties and models, embedded-SQL, active rules, consistency maintenance, serializability, concurrency control, recovery, data integration systems and federated databases, introduction to transactions in web services and workflow systems.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 3005.

courses.title: Software Quality Assurance
courses.description: Introduction to the theory and practice of Software Quality Assurance. Topics include: equivalence partitioning, test-driven testing, unit testing patterns, refactoring, software metrics, requirements engineering, scenario modeling and acceptance testing, model-based testing, state machine testing, software testing theory and tools.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3004.

courses.title: Evaluation and Research Methods for Human-Computer Interaction
courses.description: Fundamental Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) research and evaluation methods. Topics may include: HCI research methodologies, research ethics, expert evaluation, user studies, qualitative and quantitative data collection, statistical data analysis, information visualization, and specifying practical implications of findings.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2008, COMP 3008, and STAT 2509 with a minimum grade of C- in each.

courses.title: Programming for Clusters and Multi-Core Processors
courses.description: Introduction to parallel architectures, programming languages and algorithms for processor clusters and multi-core processors. Distributed memory architectures, cluster computing, message passing parallel programming, multi-core processors, shared memory parallel programming, use of thread libraries, parallel performance analysis.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402 and (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and COMP 2804.

courses.title: Introduction to Reinforcement Learning
courses.description: Learn about designing and programming reinforcement learning agents to perform complex tasks in interactive environments. Topics include Markov decision processes, dynamic programming methods, Monte Carlo methods, temporal difference learning, prediction/control with function approximation, policy gradient, and deep reinforcement learning algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2402, (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110), (MATH 1004 or MATH 1007), (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107), and (DATA 1517 or STAT 2507).

courses.title: Computer Vision
courses.description: The basic ideas and techniques of computer vision. The central theme is reconstructing 3D models from 2D images. Topics include: image formation, image feature extraction, camera models, camera calibration, structure from motion, stereo, recognition, augmented reality, image searching.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Neural Networks
courses.description: An introduction to neural networks and deep learning. Theory and application of Neural Networks to problems in machine learning. Various network architectures will be discussed. Methods for improving optimization and generalization of neural networks. Neural networks for unsupervised learning.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3105 or SYSC 4415) and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Computer Systems Security
courses.description: Information security in computer and communications systems. Topics include: design principles; operating system security and access control; web and software security; malicious software, security infrastructure; secure email; network authentication; firewalls; intrusion detection; IP security; network attacks; wireless security.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2108 and (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001).

courses.title: Data Management for Business Intelligence
courses.description: Application of computational techniques to support business activities, such as decision making, business understanding, data analysis, business process automation, learning from data, producing and using data-centric business models, ontology-based data access and integration, data quality assessment and cleaning and use of contextual data.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3005.

courses.title: Quantum Computing and Information
courses.description: Introduction to the ideas and principles of quantum computing and information. Review of mathematical foundations. Discussion of quantum theory, architecture, and quantum gates. Basic algorithms in quantum computing. Theoretical computer science and computation. Applications of quantum computing to cryptography. Quantum information and error correction.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804.

courses.title: Introduction to Natural Language Processing
courses.description: Introduction to the fundamental techniques and models of modern natural language processing. Topics include: word embedding, language models, machine translation, self-attention and transformer, question answering, and pre-trained models.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3105 or SYSC 4415) and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Multiagent Systems
courses.description: Multiagent systems is a branch of artificial intelligence that explores the interactions between multiple rational entities, where each may have access to different information and possibly conflicting priorities. This course takes an approach founded on economic game theory.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3106.

courses.title: Computational Aspects of Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Through recent advances in navigation systems, mobile devices, and new software such as Mapquest and Google Earth, GIS is becoming increasingly important and exciting from a CS perspective. This course lays the algorithmic foundations to understand, use and further this technology.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3804 or MATH 3804.

courses.title: Wireless Networks and Security
courses.description: An introduction to wireless networks covering both networking issues and security aspects of modern wireless environments. Fundamentals of mobile LANs, ad hoc, sensor networks, secure routing, searching, clustering, multicasting, localization, mobile IP/TCP, confidentiality, key establishment, authentication, broadcasting, RFIDs, and rogue attacks.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602.

courses.title: Evolving Information Networks
courses.description: Convergence of social and technological networks. Interplay between information content, entities creating it and technologies supporting it. Structure and analysis of such networks, models abstracting their properties, techniques link analysis, search, mechanism design, power laws, cascading, clustering and connections with work in social sciences.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 1805, (COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Advanced Facilities for Real-Time Games
courses.description: A practical course on the design and implementation of modern game engines and advanced facilities provided by these engines. Such facilities include systems for rendering 3D scenes; simulating physics; playing animations; game AI; and enabling multi-player games. Students will undertake a significant game development project.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3501.

courses.title: Computer Graphics
courses.description: An overview of computer graphics, with an emphasis on rendering. Topics include the following: visibility; ray tracing; graphics hardware; object and scene modeling; lighting, shading, and texture; animation.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2401 with a minimum grade of C-, COMP 2402, (MATH 1004 or MATH 1007), and (MATH 1104 or MATH 1107).

courses.title: Intelligent Web-based Information Systems
courses.description: Introduction to the principles and practice of creation, delivery and analysis of multimedia content in web-based systems. Topics include analysis of webs of documents, social network analysis, recommender systems and problems of trust, reputation and influence in e-commerce systems.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or COMP 2601 or SYSC 4504).

courses.title: Social Networking
courses.description: Introduction to virtual communities, overlay networks and social networking. Topics include architectural principles for heterogeneous social networking platforms, trust and reputation as social concepts, agent-based computing, and extraction of trends and patterns from information exchanged between community members.
courses.prereqRaw: ((COMP 2404 or SYSC 3010 or SYSC 3110) and (COMP 2406 or SYSC 4504)) or COMP 2601.

courses.title: Computing, Society, and Ethics
courses.description: This course examines ethical questions raised by computing technologies - both motivated by recent developments and through the lens of fiction. Students will identify possible ethical issues in future technologies and use formal ethics frameworks to evaluate the merits and pitfalls of different solutions.
courses.prereqRaw: Any two of COMP 2108, COMP 3004, COMP 3005, COMP 3008, (COMP 3105 or SYSC 4415), COMP 3106, COMP 3804.

courses.title: Algorithms for Data Mining, Web, and Social Networks
courses.description: Algorithm design techniques for modern data sets arising in, for example, data mining, web analytics, search engines and social networks. Topics may include locality-sensitive hashing, data streaming, clustering, recommendation systems, link analysis, dimensionality reduction, online algorithms, social network analysis, graph partitioning, and randomized algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 2804 with a minimum grade of B+.

courses.title: Computable Functions
courses.description: Recursive functions and computability, algorithms, Church's thesis, Turing machines, computational logic, NP-completeness.
courses.prereqRaw: MATH 2100 or COMP 3805 or permission of the School.

courses.title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms II
courses.description: A second course on the design and analysis of algorithms. Topics include: advanced recurrence relations, algebraic complexity, advanced graph algorithms, amortized analysis, algorithms for NP-complete problems, randomized algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3804 or MATH 3804.

courses.title: Theory of Automata
courses.description: Finite automata and regular expressions, properties of regular sets, context-free grammars, pushdown automata, deterministic context-free languages. Turing machines, the Chomsky hierarchy. Undecidability, intractable problems.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3805 or MATH 3106 or MATH 3158 (or MATH 3100) or permission of the School.

courses.title: Scientific Computing II (Honours)
courses.description: Computational methods in numerical linear algebra including solvers for large linear systems, numerical optimization and eigenvalue problems. Application to topics such as scientific machine learning or parallel computing. Focus on implementation in an efficient compiled language and standard numerical libraries.
courses.prereqRaw: i) MATH 3806; and ii) MATH 2152 or MATH 2107 with a grade of C- or higher, or permission of the school.

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science
courses.description: Advanced topics in Computer Science offered by members of the School of Computer Science.
courses.prereqRaw: permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Directed Studies
courses.description: Independent study under the supervision of a member of the School of Computer Science. Students are required to obtain their supervisor's written approval prior to registration and are limited to two such courses in their program.
courses.prereqRaw: permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Honours Project
courses.description: Under the supervision of a faculty member, Honours students complete a major Computer Science project in fourth year. Permission to register is granted once an approved project proposal is submitted to the Department. See deadlines and details on the School website.
courses.prereqRaw: fourth-year standing in an Honours program and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Honours Thesis
courses.description: Independent research under the direct supervision of a faculty advisor. Permission to register is granted once an approved thesis proposal is submitted to the School of Computer Science. Evaluation is based on a written thesis and a poster presentation.
courses.prereqRaw: fourth-year standing in an Honours program with a minimum CGPA of 9.0 in the major and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3911 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: The internship exposes students to industrial software development via placement in a local enterprise. The course may only be taken by students participating in one of the School's industrial partnerships and can only be used as a free elective in the B.C.S. program.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 4910 and registration in internship option.

courses.title: Cryptographic Algorithms and Protocols
courses.description: Block ciphers and modes of operation; public-key encryption; cryptographic hash functions; digital signatures; password-based cryptography; randomness and guesswork; authentication and authenticated key establishment protocols; challenge-response protocols; elliptic curve cryptography; post-quantum algorithms.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 1006 or COMP 1406) with a minimum grade of C-, (COMP 2804 or STAT 2507), and MATH 1104.

courses.title: Systems Security
courses.description: Securing networked computer systems. Threat modelling. Operating system security and design principles. Access control. Software-based exploits, memory safety, non-functional testing in software development. Social engineering. Browser-server and transport-layer security. Middleperson attacks, end-to-end security. Public-key certificates. Case study: Bluetooth or Wi-Fi security.
courses.prereqRaw: CSEC 2108, (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001), and (COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602).

courses.title: Operating Systems Security
courses.description: The course examines past, present, and emerging approaches for securing operating systems. The focus is to provide a foundation for understanding requirements to secure hosts at the operating system level and survey the landscape of available tools and techniques for implementing operating system security controls.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001) and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Human Factors in Security
courses.description: Designing security mechanisms with human factors in mind. Evaluating software-based systems with focus on how interaction design affects security/privacy. Current approaches to usable security; user studies; methodologies for empirical analysis; design principles for usable security/privacy; case studies including authentication, anonymity systems.
courses.prereqRaw: COMP 3008 and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Network Security
courses.description: Security throughout network stack layers. Internet core security. VPNs and tunnelling protocols. Firewalls and Intrusion Detection Systems. Internet measurements. IoT security. Botnets. Securing network protocols, including email and web. Network monitoring. Traffic sniffers and vulnerability scanners.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001), (COMP 3203 or SYSC 4602), and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Software Security
courses.description: Resilience of everyday software to vulnerabilities. Security engineering and the security development lifecycle. Static analysis and vulnerability analysis. Model checkers. Security testing, non-functional testing, fuzz-testing. Programming languages and security. Cryptographic APIs and use of security toolkits.
courses.prereqRaw: (COMP 3000 or SYSC 4001), COMP 3004 and CSEC 3108.

courses.title: Selected Topics in Security I
courses.description: An in-depth study of selected topics, with an emphasis on areas of strong current interest in research or practice.
courses.prereqRaw: CSEC 3108, and permission of the School of Computer Science.

courses.title: Selected Topics in Security II
courses.description: An in-depth study of selected topics, with an emphasis on areas of strong current interest in research or practice.
courses.prereqRaw: CSEC 3108, and permission of the School of Computer Science.
